data.title: Copper RRS 2025 - BHP's Reserves Base Provides 27 Years Of Future Output
data.description:
S&P Global Commodity Insights reports on the world's top copper producers in 2024 and their reserve replacement strategies over the 2015–24 period, following the previous Copper Reserves Replacement Strategies (RRS) articles published in 2021. These studies examine the relationships between each company's production, reserves, costs, exploration budgets, acquisitions, divestitures and copper discoveries during the period. The strategies of each of the top five producers, starting with Corporación Nacional del Cobre de Chile (Codelco) and BHP Group Ltd. (BHP), are presented individually. A follow-up article will examine the top 10 producers' strategies and rankings across various RRS metrics.

Our metrics calculations and analysis of BHP's activities primarily cover the 10-year period through the company's fiscal year ended June 2024, although the following commentary contains references to the June 2025 fiscal year.

The take

  • In 2024, BHP produced 1.3 million metric tons of attributable copper cathode and concentrate, marking the company's highest output in 15 years.
  • Between 2015 and 2024, BHP replaced 80% of its copper reserves lost to production primarily through exploration, which accounted for 64% of its reserves growth during the period.
  • Based on 2024 fiscal year data, BHP held 39.2 MMt of attributable proven and probable copper reserves, equivalent to 27 years of projected future output.
  • The Escondida and Spence mines continue to drive near-term copper production despite declining grades pressuring future output. Thus, BHP is investing further in brownfield projects to offset losses and sustain long-term growth.
  • BHP's acquisition of OZ Minerals Pty. Ltd. in 2023 brought 4.1 MMt of copper reserves through properties such as Carrapateena and Prominent Hill, while the recent acquisition of the Filo del Sol and Josemaria projects further strengthens its reserves base.

BHP has reinforced its status as a leading copper producer by expanding its reserves portfolio through exploration, early-stage project development and strategic acquisitions. From 2015 to 2024, BHP replaced a total of 11.2 MMt of copper reserves, or 80% of reserves depleted by production over the decade. As of fiscal year 2024, the company held 39.2 MMt of attributable proven and probable copper reserves, representing the fifth-largest copper reserve base globally and providing approximately 27 years of projected future output.

Near-term growth supported by acquisitions

In May 2023, BHP completed its acquisition of Australia-based OZ Minerals for $5.94 billion, turning over 4.1 MMt of copper reserves at an average cost of 66 US cents/lb. This big-ticket acquisition, which was the only copper-focused acquisition completed by BHP between 2015 and 2024, represented 36% of BHP's reserves growth over the period.

In 2024, BHP made a $33.39 billion bid for Anglo American PLC, which holds 48.4 MMt of attributable copper reserves from its Chilean copper assets Collahuasi, El Soldado and Los Bronces. The offer was ultimately rejected after several rounds of bidding.

In January 2025, BHP and Lundin Mining Corp. jointly acquired Toronto Stock Exchange-listed Filo Corp. and formed the joint venture Vicuña Corp. to manage the early-stage projects Filo del Sol and Josemaria in the Vicuña district of Argentina and Chile. With the stake split evenly, the $3.71 billion partnership brought over a total of 2.02 MMt of copper in reserves to BHP.

More recently, in August 2025, BHP announced the sale of its smaller Carajás copper assets in Brazil to CoreX Holding BV for $465 million, allowing BHP to reallocate capital to its higher-margin, larger-scale copper assets in favorable jurisdictions.

BHP turns to strategic exploration to support its long-term reserve base

Exploration activities contributed the most to BHP's reserves growth between 2015 and 2024, accounting for about 64% of the total, or 7.1 MMt of copper in reserves. Over the 10-year period, BHP allocated a cumulative $1 billion to its copper exploration budget, putting average cost for attributable copper produced at 4 cents/lb. Of the total budget, 57% targeted grassroots copper exploration. In 2024, the company reported exploration success when it disclosed the maiden resource estimates for the Oak Dam and OD Deeps deposits at Olympic Dam in Australia.

Since 1990, BHP has been credited with 16 copper discoveries, totaling 123.8 MMt of copper in reserves, resources and past production. However, the company made no new copper discoveries between 2021 and 2024 and instead focused on advancing its latest find, West Musgrave (Succoth) in Australia. Discovered in 2013, West Musgrave was regained through the OZ Minerals acquisition.

To expand its reach beyond internal exploration, BHP established Xplor, a six-month accelerator program that provides non-dilutive grants to exploration companies targeting critical minerals. Project locations of recently announced partners include Argentina, Canada, Germany, Peru, Saudi Arabia and the US.

Development projects continue to support BHP's production growth

BHP owns 57.5% of Escondida, the world's largest copper mine by output. Escondida produced 647,100 metric tons of copper in fiscal year 2024, up 7% year over year. BHP announced in the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2025 that its Full SaL leaching technology — designed to simplify leach operations and improve metal recoveries and cycle time — delivered its first production at Escondida. This innovation is expected to produce 410,000 metric tons of copper cathodes over a 10-year period. In fiscal year 2025, Escondida copper production grew 16% year over year to 750,300 metric tons, its highest output in 17 years.

BHP's wholly owned Spence mine in Chile, its second-largest copper mine and part of the Pampa Norte operations, saw output rise 6% year over year to 254,500 metric tons in 2024 on the back of better concentrator performance and feed grades. Cerro Colorado, also part of Pampa Norte, entered temporary care and maintenance in December 2023 due to mine depletion. BHP is exploring a potential restart for Cerro Colorado through the application of its SaL 1 leaching technology.

Following the OZ Minerals deal, the Carrapateena and Prominent Hill mines were incorporated into BHP's Copper South Australia operations — contributing 16% to its total concentrate production in the 2023–24 period. BHP is also leveraging cost-effective synergies and expecting improvements, including concentrate processing and expanded ore pass capacity at Olympic Dam, as well as the completion of the Wira Shaft at Prominent Hill.

Meanwhile, development at BHP's non-operated Resolution copper mine in Arizona is facing delays due to an injunction by the US Court of Appeals. This halts the transfer of federal lands held sacred by tribal nations.

In the near term, BHP expects that lower grades from its Chilean mining operations will pressure overall copper output. Despite these challenges, BHP remains focused on maintaining strong output through targeted operational improvements and strategic developments, supported by its robust reserve base. As of fiscal year 2025, the company is targeting 2 MMt per annum of attributable copper production by 2030.

data.title: Medical Properties Trust: Latest Dividend Raise Foretells Stability (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
MPW stock: Q3 earnings report and latest dividend declaration

I last analyzed Medical Properties Trust (MPW) stock on 9.21 with an article titled "Medical Properties Trust: Sell The Rally (Technical Analysis)". That article focused on the trading patterns at that time (which showed the stock was overbought) and rated it as a sell. Since then, there have been a few material developments evolving around this medical REIT company. In the remainder of this article, I will concentrate on the top 2 on my list: the recent release of its FQ3 earnings report [ER] and also its latest dividend declarations. These developments have led me to form a more stable outlook for its profits and also stock prices, which in turn led me to upgrade its rating to hold.

Let me start with the Q3 ER. As seen in the screenshot below, the company reported mixed earnings for FQ3 on Oct 30, 2025. Both FFO and EPS missed consensus estimates by a good margin. However, upon closer look, the picture is not as bleak as it seems on the surface for reasons detailed in the next section. Looking ahead, consensus FFO projection points to $0.15, translating into a robust QoQ recovery of 15.3%. I have good confidence for such an upbeat FFO forecast to materialize judging by its latest dividend declarations as elaborated immediately below.

MPW stock: latest dividend declarations

As another key development since my last writing, MPW also announced its dividend payouts for the fourth quarter of 2025. As seen in the table below, the payout, with an ex-dividend date of Dec 11, 2025, is declared to be $0.09 per share. This represents a 12.5% increase from the previous regular quarterly dividend of $0.08 per share, quite close to the projected FFO estimate quoted above.

This reminds me of the old adage that says the safest payout is the one that has just been raised. I believe this adage applies particularly well in the case of MPW, giving its REIT status (which means it pays out the majority of its economic profit as dividends) and also its recent transitions. In particular, despite the double-digit increase, the new payouts are well supported in my model. As mentioned above, current consensus projection points to an FFO of $0.15 for FQ4. At the new payout rate of $0.09 per share, this implies a payout ratio of only 60% for FQ4. For the previous quarter, the payout ratio was about 61.5% at a dividend of $0.08 per share and a normalized FFO of $0.13 per share. To better contextualize these figures, the second chart below shows MPW's cash payout ratio in the past 5 years. You can see how irregular its dividend coverage has been in recent years, and why I believe the forward coverage ratio of around ~60% is a strong signal for stability ahead.

MPW stock: dividend cushion ratio

Readers following our writings must know that we never make our thesis based on simple coverage ratios. These payout ratios are, of course, always good starting points, but readers should be aware of their fundamental flaws. Among all their limitations, these ratios ignored the balance sheet of the company, i.e., the financial assets and obligations of the stock (such as debt, payables, cash, receivables, etc.), which are especially relevant in the case of MPW, in our view. I will examine its leverage ratios in the risk section, which will show why these limitations are of particular relevance.

In a nutshell, we need to decide if the stability in its payout ratios is offset - of even outweighed - by deterioration of its balance sheet strength. The approach we use to make this assessment is the so-called dividend cushion ratio ("DCR"). This approach is detailed in Brian M Nelson's book, Value Trap. A recap of the gist is quoted below for ease of reference:

The DCR is obtained by A) summing the existing net cash a company has on its balance sheet plus its expected future free cash flows over the next five years, and B) dividing that sum by future expected cash dividends (including expected growth in them, where applicable) over the same time period. If the ratio is significantly above 1, the company generally has sufficient financial capacity to pay out its expected future dividends. The higher the ratio, the better, all else equal.

Following the above steps, my calculations for MPW's DCR are shown in the figure below. Before commenting on my calculations, let me first point a deviation from Nelson's above recipe in my calculation here. In step A, Nelson's recipe calls for the subtraction of all the liabilities to obtain the net cash position. But for a REIT business like MPW, I only subtracted the current liabilities (mostly its accounts payable) and also the interest expenses. Also in step A, instead of using the free cash flow, I used its fund from operations, given its REIT nature.

With these two deviations, MPW has been maintaining a DCR that far exceeds the 1x minimum threshold mentioned above. To wit, its DCR averages around 1.83x in the past 7 years. My 2025 estimate was obtained under these assumptions: A) a quarterly payout of $0.09 per share, and B) a forward FFO of $0.55 per share for full FY 2025 per the latest consensus estimate. With these parameters, the bad news is that MPW's DCR has weakened rather drastically from its recent historical levels. But on the positive side, the projected DCR is still around 1.50x. It is well above the 1x safety threshold and also represents an improvement above the 2024 reading of around 1.46x.

Other risks and final thoughts

The above deterioration of DCR is caused by a few key factors. First, its FFO has been erratic and suffered setbacks in recent quarters with the trouble it had with key tenants. Second, the company's balance sheet has also become more stretched in tandem. As an example, the top panel of the next chart below shows its debt-to-assets ratio in the past 5 years, from 2021 through 2025. As seen, the ratio has increased substantially and currently hovers around 64.43%. This reading is significantly above the period's average of 55.98% and is also the highest level in at least 5 years. As another reflection of its more stretched balance sheet, the bottom panel shows its debt-to-equity ratio in this period. As seen, the trend here mirrors what we just saw in the top panel, showing a dramatic rise from around 1x in early 2021 to the current level of 3.154x. Due to such a large change in its capital structure and also the FFO decrease, the DCR has become weakened as a result despite the large dividend cuts the company has started in 2H of 2023 (see the last chart below).

Against this backdrop, I believe the latest dividend raise of 12.5% is especially noteworthy. Together with the projected FFO recovery and the normalization of its payout ratio, this raise could mark a turning point for the company's fundamentals. The dividend safety - and also future growth potential - is dampened to some degree by the weakening of the DCR. But overall, I see an improved return/risk curve compared to the time of my last writing, resulting in a rating adjustment to hold.

data.title: 2026 Fixed-Income Outlook: Seizing Opportunities In A Rate-Cutting Cycle
data.description:
By Gene Tannuzzo, CFA & Ed Al-Hussainy

Prospects for bonds in 2026 look strong but not without hurdles. With proactive rate cuts by the Federal Reserve (Fed), resilient corporate fundamentals and continued investor appetite for fixed income, conditions support compelling returns. Still, a weakening labor market and tight credit spreads present potential challenges. We see 2026 shaping up to be a year where investors can find value by locking in yield, managing duration and focusing on diversification — while keeping a close eye on credit quality.

Positioning for proactive Fed rate cuts

We are in an environment where the Fed is proactively cutting rates to remove the risk of negative outcomes, not in reaction to a crisis (i.e., recession). The market is currently pricing in a sequence of cuts totaling 75–100 basis points (bps) in 2025 and an additional 75 bps in 2026. This suggests the market expects an aggressive cycle of rate cuts, which would be a significant departure from historical norms outside of a recession (see table below).

To deliver this level of easing, the Fed would require further evidence of labor market deterioration, and this evidence will need to arrive relatively soon. It also requires the Fed to look through (overlook) any direct and secondary effects of tariffs on inflation, the acceleration in private sector capital spending, the easing of financial conditions and the positive fiscal impact of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act.

We believe this scenario is unlikely, which suggests the front end of the Treasury curve is mispriced — creating an opportunity for investors. And unlike previous cycles, where rate cuts steepened the yield curve, today’s cuts are likely to keep the curve stable or slightly flatter. This creates three implications for fixed-income portfolios:

  • Duration becomes attractive. It offers both more yield and downside protection against equity market drawdowns, especially relative to cash.
  • Income harvesting. Investors can capture elevated starting yields without needing to take a directional bet on the economy.
  • Diversification. High-quality fixed income provides a buffer, particularly in an environment where inflation remains relatively subdued.

Our playbook: Finding value with falling rates, tight spreads and strong fundamentals

The tension between labor market softness and ongoing economic growth will define the bond market's path in 2026. We believe three scenarios could play out:

In our base case scenario, with 10-year Treasury yields around 4% and investment-grade credit yielding near 5%, bonds present a compelling value proposition, especially with inflation at around 3%. In this environment, investors should prioritize sectors offering higher yield per unit of duration.

These factors point to specific areas of opportunity within fixed income:

  • Consumer loans. Asset-based finance stands out as an area of value. Backed by healthy household balance sheets and secured with collateral, consumer-oriented bonds offer diversification away from traditional corporate credit.
  • Investment grade. Fundamentals are solid, but elevated prices make corporate bonds less compelling due to the risk of spread widening. For institutional investors, they remain an important asset class for liability matching. Agency mortgage-backed securities offer investment-grade quality at a better value.
  • Artificial intelligence (AI). The massive buildout of AI infrastructure is reshaping credit markets, creating new opportunities for bond investors through innovative funding structures and increased capital demand.
  • International bonds. Non-U.S. growth may begin to look more attractive, with steeper yield curves offering additional risk premium in markets like Japan, France and Australia. There are pockets of value in emerging market debt.
  • Leveraged loans. This is a contrarian call, given tight spreads and falling rates. However, they are not as expensive as other sectors and have matured into a through-the-cycle product, extending the high-yield opportunity set.

At the same time, we remain relatively risk-averse in sectors where we think we are not compensated enough for risk.

Managing portfolio risk in a tight credit environment

There are growing downside risks to the “steady as you go” outlook for 2026:

  • Credit dispersion. Recent high-profile defaults were issuer-specific, reflecting weakness at the bottom end of both household and corporate borrowers. Importantly, we are not seeing that weakness spread. Instead, the real story is dispersion: After several years of monitoring, we are now seeing meaningful differences in credit performance across sectors and issuers.
  • Tariffs. Another potential risk is tariffs. While the economic reaction has been muted so far, the effects may be working through inventories slowly and could hit consumers more forcefully in 2026.
  • Credit volatility. Investors should also be prepared for volatility in spreads as the cycle matures. Focus on real risks — labor market trends, consumer stress, and tariff impacts — while tuning out the noise. This is when and where fundamental credit research shines.
  • Policy and politics. Policy-related noise has been intense and is expected to remain so in 2026. However, investors should keep in mind that this has limited influence on the actual performance of fixed income markets. Demand for U.S. fixed-income continues to be strong, as many policy themes have not significantly impacted market dynamics.

The bottom line

In 2026, we believe the bond market will continue to offer value and opportunity — but not without risk. The Fed’s preemptive cuts, a stable macroeconomic backdrop and healthy demand for fixed income set the stage for constructive returns. Yet, vigilance is warranted as labor market stress and credit events could become more pronounced. By focusing on duration, yield and diversification, investors can position portfolios to weather volatility and capture opportunity in a changing landscape.

data.title: Franklin New York Tax-Free Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: In the third quarter of 2025 (Q3), new issuance of municipal (muni) bonds exceeded the average seen in the past few years, driven by a 34% year-over-year surge in July issuance. By quarter end, the yield on the benchmark 10-year US Treasuries (UST) note fell by eight basis points (bps) to 4.15%, while the 30-year UST yield decreased by four bps to 4.73%. US markets rose on clearer tariff policies, economic resilience and a more dovish Fed, which cut rates by 25 bps to 4.00%-4.25% in September.
  • Contributors: Overweight allocation to muni bonds with 20 or more years to maturity, security selection in AA rated bonds and overweight allocation to A rated bonds.
  • Detractors: Overweight allocation to BBB rated bonds and underweight allocation to muni bonds with two to ﬁve years to maturity.
  • Outlook: Municipal bond valuations continue to appear attractive, particularly on a tax-adjusted basis. In a more challenging environment, strong bottom-up research and disciplined security selection will be essential to identifying relative value and preserving portfolio quality.

Performance Review

  • Over the quarter, Franklin New York Tax-Free Income Fund (FNYTX) outperformed its benchmark, the Linked Bloomberg Muni Bond New York Exempt/Muni Bond Index.
  • The fund’s yield curve positioning contributed to relative performance during the third quarter, primarily driven by our overweight to muni bonds with 20 or more years to maturity. However, our underweight to muni bonds with two to ﬁve years to maturity curbed returns.
  • Rating allocations detracted from relative returns for the period, owing to our overweight to BBB rated bonds. Conversely, our overweight to A rated bonds partially lifted results. Security selection benefited relative results, particularly due to selection in AA and BBB rated securities.

Outlook

  • Muni bond issuance exceeded historical averages in Q3 as issuers, who had been reluctant to issue new bonds under the looming threat to their tax-exempt status, came to market. Positive fund flows into the asset class were not able to keep up with the supply. The muni bond yield curve continues to steepen, with longer-maturity bond valuations cheapening somewhat.
  • Fundamentals remain broadly stable, however, as the economy slows and inflationary pressures ease, tax revenue growth at the state and local level is expected to moderate, placing greater importance on credit selectivity going forward.
  • Municipal bond valuations continue to appear attractive, particularly on a tax-adjusted basis. Yields are near multi-year highs, allowing investors to lock in compelling income. In a more challenging environment, strong bottom-up research and disciplined security selection will be essential to identifying relative value and preserving portfolio quality.

data.title: Archer's Midnight In The UAE: Early Revenue Could Reach $250M
data.description:
Thesis

Archer Aviation Inc. (ACHR) is developing the Midnight, and investors need it to come sooner rather than later. It’s tipped to be a four-passenger eVTOL designed for fast, quiet, zero-emission urban air travel. We should see the company plan for a hybrid business model to generate revenue through aircraft sales and also via operating air-taxi services. Now, whilst the aircraft is in pre-revenue and still in FAA and UAE GCAA certification, Archer is already advancing commercial readiness, with its first operations planned in the UAE. I’m estimating that a 50-aircraft fleet there could generate about $250 million in sales and $2.5 to 12.5 million in annual ride revenue as an early start. This, of course, depends on adoption. In recent times, the stock has seen quite a big pullback from the $13 mark we saw in mid-October. You can now pick up shares in the mid-$7 range, and I’m seeing this as a good entry point or an opportunity to buy the pullback. It's at a 33% discount over the past month, as you can see on the chart below. I'm quite bullish on what we could see from Archer in 2026 and see more upside than negatives from here.

What they do

Archer is a pretty advanced aerospace company. They focus on developing electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) aircraft designed to be very fast, quiet, and have zero emissions in urban transportation. As you know, their main aircraft, Midnight, is a pilot-operated, four-passenger air taxi. It’s built for short, frequent hops around cities, such as a run to the airport. Midnight is yet to be commercially available, but the company is working through the FAA’s multi-stage certification process to bring it into service.

Their business model as of now actually combines a few different revenue streams: first, selling Midnight aircraft to anyone from airlines, governments, and mobility operators; second, operating its own air-taxi network and generating recurring revenue from high-frequency passenger flights; third, maintenance, repair, and overhaul services for its own fleet and customer-owned aircraft. They also do pilot training, which is enabled by their FAA Part 135 and Part 141 certifications.

As you would expect with such a stealthy aircraft, Archer also has some military interest. They do have defence contracts, through which Archer supplies powertrains and VTOL technology for military applications. They also generate some revenue via technology licensing, monetising its own proprietary electric powertrain, battery systems, flight-control software, and related IP. Now, Archer currently remains pre-revenue and actually burns quite a bit of capital during development; however, its long-term model is built around combining those high-margin aircraft sales with recurring operational revenues. What the company eventually wants is a vertically integrated ecosystem that covers manufacturing, operations, infrastructure partnerships, and defence-related applications as a nice revenue bonus.

3Q25

For 3Q25, Archer reported a net loss of roughly $129.9 million, up from the $115.3 million we saw a year earlier. Operating expenses for the quarter were approximately $174.8 million, and the company recorded an adjusted EBITDA loss of about US$116.1 million. The company also managed to end the quarter with cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments of roughly $1.641 billion, which was boosted earlier in the year by a $650 million equity raise.

A step closer to commercialisation

The reason this stock has gained such popularity in recent times is because of Midnight; each quarter, investors get updates that show the aircraft even closer to commercialisation. It’s going to be the biggest catalyst for Archer once they get successful certification and launch commercial operations. Now the closest place they’re set to do that is in the UAE. It’s set to be the company’s first real opportunity to generate some meaningful recurring revenue. Now, Archer has already deployed its first Midnight aircraft to Abu Dhabi and has begun local flight testing, pilot training, and other operational readiness work. It’s being conducted alongside the Abu Dhabi Aviation and Etihad’s training organisation. Now, to operate commercially in the UAE, Midnight must pass the UAE GCAA’s type certification process. As you would expect with a UAE certification, it closely mirrors the FAA’s requirements for things like airworthiness, performance, safety systems, redundancy, and other maintenance standards. The company knows how important the progress is to investors and actually laid out the FAA progress route pretty clearly in the presentation. You can get a sense of just how long the process can be in the chart below.

The good news here is that the UAE wants the aircraft approved; they want the popularity and novelty of having those taxi flights across the country. So, because the UAE is aggressively pushing to be the world’s first eVTOL-operational region, the regulatory pathway is moving a lot faster than in the U.S. If they’re certified, what kind of revenue would we be looking at for the flights? Well, it's going to depend a lot on uptake, but I see a lot of early commercial routes that should be short-distance, high-frequency urban flights. For starters, we have Abu Dhabi City to Yas Island, Saadiyat Island, the airport, and the Dubai corridors. These are all typical trip lengths of 10 to 25 miles. There are also routes that are specifically optimised for Midnight’s rapid-turnaround capabilities, so we’re talking 10-minute recharges and having multiple flights per hour back and forth.

Modelling revenue in the UAE

Now, whilst there is no publicly disclosed exact cost to make a Midnight aircraft, here’s what we can estimate based on what we know. Archer’s catalogue/indicative selling price for Midnight is about $5 million per aircraft, and in its 2Q24 shareholder letter, Archer also assumes a $5 million sale price when modelling its target production economics. From their filings, we also know the company’s goal is to reach 40% to 50% gross margin per aircraft in future production. Elsewhere, to scale manufacturing, Stellantis is committing up to $400 million to support the mass production of Midnight. So if Archer can hit its target gross margin, the cost to make a Midnight could be in the ballpark of $2.5 million to $3 million per aircraft, though that’s an estimate based on their projections. So, based on a $5 million sale price per Midnight and an estimated production cost of $2.5 to $3 million, we can estimate that a UAE fleet of about 50 aircraft could generate around $250 million in sales. There would be roughly $110 to $115 million in gross profit.

Now, it is still early days, but it is assumed Archer is set to use a hybrid model in the UAE, so both selling/leasing aircraft and supporting the ride operations. Based on UAE tourism and intercity travel, the initial demand for eVTOL rides on routes that I mentioned earlier, we can estimate a market capture of about 1% to 5% of relevant passenger trips. The UAE is expected to host 27.6 million international visitors in 2025. Abu Dhabi is targeting 39.3 million visitors annually by 2030, and Dubai saw 18.72 million international overnight visitors in 2024. If we assume 5 million passenger trips to those hot spots per year, it would be roughly 50,000 to 250,000 rides per year for a 50-aircraft fleet. At an average fare of $50, this translates to $2.5 to 12.5 million in annual ride revenue. So what's clear here is that the actual uptake is very dependent on vertiport availability, their pricing, and traveller adoption of the new service. I think traveller adoption in the UAE will be strong; there is a lot of hype around Midnight taking flight. So what changes things is the price per flight figure and how many rides they can squeeze into the year.

Headwinds

Now, it won't be easy sailing for Archer. They may, of course, face some regulatory delays in FAA or GCAA certification since they're aiming to push Midnight down the pathway so quickly. The certifications are actually quite a complex, multi-stage process that requires Midnight to demonstrate strict compliance with airworthiness, safety, performance, and maintenance standards. If we did see a delay here and the FAA needed additional testing requirements, then it would push back commercial launch timelines, and the stock would see some downside as a result.

Valuation

Archer currently trades at $7.42 per share and holds a market cap of about $5.13 billion. It gives an enterprise value of $3.58 billion. The company also has quite minimal debt at just $88.6 million and strong cash reserves, with that $1.64 billion figure dominating the balance sheet. It’s quite a solid liquidity position. Now, they’re yet to have any meaningful revenue, so traditional earnings-based metrics aren’t quite yet meaningful. But on a trailing twelve-month basis, its price-to-book ratio of 3.10x tells us the market values the company at a modest premium to its book value.

Archer shares, until recently, had quite a lot of expectations baked into the stock when it was trading at the mid-$13. However, since October, we’ve actually seen a pullback across a lot of defence-related stocks, and Archer is no exception. Shares have pulled back into that mid-$7 range, and I’m viewing this as a good discount for investors. Approval is coming closer for the UAE, and I expect that to be the next big stock mover. Afterwards, it is a bit of unknown in terms of what we will see for order numbers or pricing/demand. So, whilst the valuation still reflects a bit of investor expectations for future growth in the eVTOL market, I think with the pullback back the risk is lowered significantly.

Conclusion

So to sum up, with the 33% pullback in stock price over the last month, I'm seeing Archer as much more de-risked than some other small aviation players. The next big thing to watch will be certification in the UAE. I'm expecting this to see significant positive momentum in the stock. The other good news is that they hold a pretty strong cash position, so once commercialisation is a go, they'll be in a good position to market the flights.

data.title: Ferrovial: Impressive Growth, But Priced For Perfection
data.description:
I think that Ferrovial (FER) is an interesting opportunity to get exposure to the “European” infrastructure industry (most of the activity actually comes from North America). The recent Q3 2025 results are positive and paint an attractive growth outlook for the company, driven by a portfolio of high-quality assets.

A strong driver is the Highways division, with the operations in North America, showing impressive revenues and EBITDA thanks to market dynamics and good traffic management.

The balance sheet of the company appears to be exceptionally healthy, and the capital allocation is strictly disciplined, bringing risks down. The pipeline of new projects all over the world makes it an attractive, diversified infrastructure company, and the company's commitment to shareholder returns makes it very attractive for both income and growth investors, if bought at a reasonable valuation.

I give Ferrovial a hold rating. The company, thanks to its really encouraging returns this year, is priced at a premium, and there is not much room for upside left for now.

The North American Toll Road Engine

The key driver of the company is its Highways division, and the most interesting part of the portfolio is the NA assets. YTD, the revenues from highway operations were up 16.4% on a like-for-like basis, bringing the EBITDA up by 15.1%. Within this division, 97% of the EBITDA is coming from NA highway assets, making up for 71% of the company’s total EBITDA. In short, while Ferrovial is considered a European company, most of its activity is coming from NA, and I think that this should be reflected in the valuations.

A significant part of the success of the division can be attributed to the 407 ETR, an express toll route in Toronto. This central road of the area saw an increase of 9.4% in traffic in Q3, 6.2% YTD. This is driven by several different factors, such as the decreasing remote work activity and the commercial initiatives aimed at boosting the demand through promotions and other initiatives. What is most impressive is that with only a 6.2% YTD increase in frequentation, the revenues YTD grew by 19.3%, pushing the EBITDA up by 15.8%. This is a testimony to the effectiveness of the promotions pushed by the company, and it led to an unexpected release of provision of CAD 9.8 Mn (€ 6.0 Mn): not a very significant figure overall, but a good way to see that the performance of the asset was better than expected. This good performance is confirmed by the increase in distributions: the asset led to CAD 450 Mn in dividends YTD, and the board approved a CAD 1.05 Bn dividend for Q4 2025 only, which is a 36% increase from the prior year.

The same trend can be seen in other assets of the region, such as in the Dallas-Fort Worth’s managed lanes that are showing both impressive growth, and the fact that the company has strong pricing power. The only disappointing (but expected due to construction operations) figure is the slight decline in traffic of the NTE, but in front of that we see a 14.2% YTD increase in revenue per transaction, indicating that revenues are going to surge as well when the construction situation is resolved. Besides the NTE, other assets such as the LBJ and the NTE 35W, not affected by construction operations, saw both an increase in traffic and in EBITDA. In Q3, the NTE 35W saw an increase of 12.0% in revenue per transaction. According to the management, this increase is driven by the higher share of heavy vehicles and very flexible pricing policies allowing them to override the soft cap.

These impressive figures are not only seen in Texas but also in Virginia with the I-66 (13.2% traffic growth in Q3) and in North Carolina with the I-77 (+24.4% in revenue per transaction and +21.1% EBITDA).

Construction and Airport Developments

In front of the strong cash-generation of the Highways division, the other segments of the company are preparing the company for its future growth.

First, the Construction division, also exhibiting a strong profitability with an EBIT margin of 3.7% YTD is in line with long-term targets, comforting about the trajectory of the company. The division operates with two subsidiaries, Budimex and Webber, both also exhibiting strong profitability metrics with EBIT margins of 7.6% and 3.0%. A key determinant for this division is the book order, and since year end 2024, it is up 9.1% LFL to stand at €17.2 Bn. Almost 50% of it is in NA. Such a strong backlog is very reassuring as it shows the strong demand benefiting the company, and the success of its concession-led model.

Next is the Airports division. For now, the key focus is to deliver the New Terminal One (NTO) at JFK airport. The budget is in line, and the project is estimated to be 78% completed, but some caution should be put about the planned delivery date of June 2026, and management said that they are looking at acceleration measures to ensure delivery on time. On my end, I would bet on some short-term delays.

However, even with delays, this doesn’t mean that the project will not be a success, and the project has already secured commitments from 21 different airlines and achieved a successful refinancing of €1.4 Bn in July through long-term debt.

A secondary focus goes to the Dalaman airport in Turkey, showing a resilient growth of +1.8% in terms of EBITDA so far. This is driven by several factors, such as commercial upgrades, while the outlook is weakened by tensions in the Middle East. This is clearly not going to be a driver of performance, but it is very reassuring to see that the asset is resilient.

A Justified Premium

To properly identify peers of Ferrovial, I don’t think that it is relevant to look at European companies. As mentioned in the previous part of this analysis, as a significant part of the business comes from North America, I think that we should be looking at peers of the region, despite the roots of the company in Europe. Valuations in Europe are lower than in NA, and if you compare them with the value of Ferrovial, it will only make you think that the company is overvalued, while it is not really the case.

Looking at North American peers, I think that Ferrovial is currently priced at a premium, which makes sense considering the exceptional results of this year, but this also indicates that there is not much room for further upside, and that the best option now is to wait for multiples to go down. The company is to be considered as a high-quality infrastructure manager & owner, rather than a simple low-margin contractor, considering the repartition between the three activities of the group.

Company/Peer Category

As you can see, Ferrovial is indeed trading at a premium. The most comparable company is Transurban Group, and the valuation of Ferrovial is roughly 7% above (average of Seeking Alpha and MarketScreener's forward-looking multiples).

I do think that it makes sense considering the description of the results I gave you above: explosive growth of US assets, operational excellence, and a strong balance sheet (see the part below). But, as you can see from the chart below showing the evolution of the share price and the P/E ratio since the beginning of the year, most of the growth is coming from the P/E multiple growing, and I don’t think that it is sustainable in the long run.

A Financial Fortress

As the toll road activity represents a very strong cash machine, this is seen in the financial position of the company. Ferrovial finished the Q3 with a net cash position of €706 Mn, not taking infrastructure project debt into account. This strong figure is driven by the company's strong ability to generate cash, its disciplined investment execution, and divestments such as the sale of a 5.25% stake in AGS Airports. As the company is pursuing growth opportunities and is ramping up its project pipeline, this gives room for execution without stressing the balance sheet.

This also allows the company to put in place a strong program of shareholder returns and growth projects. So far this year, the company has bought an additional €1.3 Bn stake in the 407 ETR, made a €239 Mn injection into the NTO project, and redistributed €426 Mn to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks. They are on track to achieve the €2.2 Bn plan of shareholder returns announced in 2024.

Everything seems to go smoothly, and this is seen in the valuation, fairly priced (with a premium).

Risks

While the outlook of the company is positive, the high valuation level warrants caution. There are many elements that could alter the positive investor sentiment, and bring valuation down to levels that we saw earlier this year.

For example, there could be a delay in the opening of JFK's Terminal One, planned for June 2026, that would postpone the cash generation of the asset. This is slightly offset by the fact that the contractor would face damages, but it would still hurt the business, and the confidence in future constructions.

In addition, the company is very dependent on traffic trends in North America, and while they have been really positive so far this year, thanks to the return-to-office wave, I don’t see the strong growth in traffic being sustainable, and there is a point at which we will see a return to reality that will temper investors' confidence. Maybe then, this will be a good time to buy Ferrovial.

Conclusion

Ferrovial is a very strong infrastructure operator with prime assets in North America and is delivering investors very impressive growth figures. However, this is already something priced generously by the market, and the company is priced at a premium against peers.

The trajectory of the company is solid, but I would rather recommend a drop in valuation multiples before considering an investment in the company, as I don’t see an upside following an immediate buy.

Overall, I do think that the company makes a good long-term holding, but the pricing is currently not leaving room for mistakes, and no company is perfect.

data.title: Hess Midstream: High-Yield Undervalued Midstream Play With Long-Term Gas Potential
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Hess Midstream LP (HESM) is the limited partnership formed by Hess Corporation [HES] to own and develop midstream assets to support oil and gas operations across the Bakken region. With the stock taking a hit recently due to factors including weaker growth expectations that ultimately stem from the issues seen in the industry, HESM got to a more than 9% distribution yield, while they keep reporting solid numbers, which I believe can offer an opportunity.

Hess Midstream used to be owned by Hess, which was recently bought by Chevron Corporation (CVX), who now owns ~37.9% interest in Hess Midstream on a consolidated basis. Unlike Hess itself, which I considered to be among the weakest US oil giants due to the bad profitability and management (in my opinion), HESM actually makes very good money. As we can see, the company’s adjusted free cash flow reached $186.8 million in Q3 2025, which is not bad at all for their ~$6.77 billion market cap.

For the whole year, HESM’s guidance hints at $760 million to $770 million in adjusted FCF, placing them at a ratio of around 8.85, which is not bad and allows them to pay a very solid yield. HESM also updated their 2025 CAPEX guidance to ~$270 million from $300 million based on the suspension of the Capa gas plant and removal of the project from its forward plan as they are adjusting to the current environment.

Financially, based on HESM’s latest report, we see something I recently highlighted for other midstreamers: the fact that their balance sheets aren’t great. The current assets don’t cover the current liabilities, while the debt is quite significant compared to the assets, leaving very little equity compared to the market cap, although it’s worth mentioning that the current value of these assets is likely higher in today’s market compared to when they were recorded and depreciated, especially since they make so much money.

This is far from the worst balance sheet though, and no notes mature until 2028 (which comes with $1.35 billion), so they should have enough time to wait before refinancing at potentially similar rates since the Fed would likely continue its rate cuts, while the leverage is not as high as in other cases.

Shareholder Returns

Hess Midstream pays a ~9% yield today, increasing it in virtually every quarter since going public. As of Q3, that’s an annualized payout of ~$627.13 million, meaning a payout ratio of ~82% based on the midpoint of their estimated Adjusted FCF, which is very good.

This allows them to do moves like what they did back in May 2025. HESM also announced a ~$200 million repurchase, including $190 million in Class B units (from its sponsors) and $10 million worth of Class A shares (from the public), a month that also marked the previous sponsor’s Global Infrastructure Partners' full exit. After this, they also went through a subsequent $100 million repurchase ($30 million from sponsors, $70 million public), after which they still expect to have about $1.25 billion of financial flexibility through 2027 for incremental shareholder returns, including more buybacks.

Risks & Opportunities

Something I mentioned when covering peers is that even though midstream is more stable and doesn’t depend directly on oil and gas prices, the latter still has indirect effects that can affect them. As HESM even saw recently when Chevron announced cutting Bakken rigs, bad times for oil and gas can lead to bad times for midstreamers as well. As we’ve proven once again, when the upstream or downstream (or both) have issues, this can impact the whole supply chain, which in this case led to weaker guidance until 2027 even though the company continues to expect long-term growth in gas throughput volumes in the region, which comes with its own profitability risks.

Because of that, we can also expect things like the US-Venezuela situation to be very impactful, which the market is definitely paying close attention to. After the US placed an aircraft carrier in the Caribbean and struck over 20 ships there, the latest development as of the moment of writing this is the US’ plan to designate a Venezuelan drug cartel it alleges is led by Maduro (Venezuela’s president) as a foreign terrorist organization, which Trump hinted to be a move that could allow the US to target certain assets and infrastructure in the country. Again, this comes despite saying he’s not planning an attack on the country a few weeks ago but also saying Maduro’s days are numbered, so it’s hard to tell what the plan is.

Again, I can’t stress enough how important this is because Venezuela is not just a random target. This is by far the number one country in terms of proven oil reserves, with roughly 4 times what the US or Russia has. As the country is also one of the founding members of OPEC, it’s also unlikely to see its allies just sit and watch the US attack whatever they want in the country. This has the potential to degenerate into something much worse than just some threats, and we’ve seen Russia saying it’s “ready” to help Venezuela’s military as they’ve been rallying troops recently, while Maduro even urged Trump to avoid a “forever war” in the region similar to the one in Afghanistan.

Similar to its peers, the main near-term catalyst will be US rate cuts, which can help in several ways, including directly lowering the company’s interest expenses ($221.1 million TTM, their highest ever), potentially unlocking a lot of growth ahead as they refinance and reduce these. Again, rate cuts can be good for the entire industry and economy as a whole, as they would lower the cost of debt, increase discretionary budgets, spending and investing, so they would’ve been beneficial even if the company had zero debt.

Also, since the company comes with a very solid yield, there’s potential to see more demand as money will have to risk up if it wants to get solid yields, as the theoretical risk-free rates would fall alongside the rates given enough time (assuming that nothing fundamental changes in the US). This increases the risk-adjusted return for a stock like this, potentially leading to better valuations.

However, rate cuts are now under pressure at least in the near term, as the US Government recently went through what became its longest shutdown in history, which also caused the BLS to halt its data collection and publishing (with a new release only in November). As a result, the Fed has less up-to-date data for its next meeting at a time when I believe we could’ve seen the effects of tariffs on top of the inflation and unemployment that Powell even mentioned during the October meeting.

On top of that, we’re looking at a lot of pressure in the market coming from the tech sell-off, as the market is now worrying (again) about Big Tech’s AI spending and profitability at a time when uncertainty was already high.

The CEO also addressed a question regarding wider Bakken Gas-Oil Ratios (GORs) trends during the latest call, saying the following:

But in general, as we've talked about, our expectation is based on the new guidance that we gave out 3 rigs that Chevron is running, we expect to maintain oil to plateau and then gas to increase over time, and that basically is driven by GORs. Because at this point, we're really at almost full gas capture. So really the trend in gas is going to be GR driven. So with that, that will really continue to drive growth for Hess Midstream over the long term, as gas represents 75% of our revenues.

That last part is very important to remember, as natural gas has very significant potential going forward given all the LNG projects and even Trump’s US-EU $750 billion LNG deal, with many companies now calling a “Third Wave” of LNG growth thanks to these moves following the war, where the US rapidly moved to take over Russia’s role as Europe’s key gas supplier.

Something with some potential is the fact that following Chevron’s acquisition of Hess, which closed in July 2025, key Hess executives stepped off while Chevron leadership joined, which can potentially be a good move and boost HESM with more experienced people.

So, HESM’s management will likely continue to face macro pressure, which can at least affect their growth targets. Meanwhile, the high rates will make things even worse, especially now that the stock has fallen quite a bit recently, which means that they have access to less efficient external capital if they want to issue stock or debt.

Especially in this environment, it’s not impossible to see Chevron or other partners continue to cut costs and invest less, which can lead to issues for companies all the way through the supply chain, potentially impacting HESM directly.

Peer Comparison

Compared to a diverse group of peers such as ONEOK, Inc. (OKE), Summit Midstream Corporation (SMC), Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD), Energy Transfer LP (ET), MPLX LP (MPLX) and Plains All American Pipeline, L.P. (PAA), we see overall solid ratios in almost every regard, with a low P/CF and some of the lowest ratios almost all around the board, which is a good sign for me.

Valuation

Starting with $765 million in Adjusted FCF (midpoint of updated guidance), let’s assume a 4% CAGR over the first 5 years as we could see more demand for the region later on, once natural gas potentially becomes more in demand, combined with rate cuts lowering their interest expenses, followed by a 3.5% CAGR in the subsequent 5 and a 2.5% terminal growth rate. I’ll assume a WACC of 9% (above my estimated ~8.3% based on a cost of equity of ~10.8% and ~5.3% cost of debt), with $3.79 billion in net debt, $146 million in non-controlling interests and 207.66 million shares outstanding (129.378 million Class A, 78.283 million Class B) as of the Q3 report. This way, we get to an enterprise value of $12.89 billion, an equity value of $8.96 billion and an intrinsic value of $43.14 per share.

Conclusions

I rate HESM a Buy given its attractive yields and long-term potential in a market that may value natural gas higher in the future, despite the near-term headwinds. Despite the near-term headwinds from political uncertainty, high interest rates, and upstream production cuts, the company’s long-term growth potential in natural gas is boosted by LNG demand and structural energy shifts, which position them well for sustainable long-term returns.

data.title: Private Credit Risks In Context
data.description:
Private credit’s rapid growth faces its first real test

Private credit has rapidly grown to be a leading source for corporate lending, representing more than $2 trillion in assets under management. However, in the wake of a few recent borrower defaults and headlines in select sectors, there has been debate as to whether these represent cracks in the system or are simply isolated events.

As the chart shows, the rise of private credit has been dramatic, underscoring how material the market has become to institutional portfolios. Yet, scale alone doesn’t make it systemic. These defaults stem largely from alleged fraud and sector-specific shocks rather than broad deterioration in credit quality.

Private credit AUM has quadrupled since 2014

AUM in USD billions

Borrowers are feeling the squeeze

The data does show some softening in borrower fundamentals. Interest coverage ratios have declined, free cash flow has weakened, and the share of payment-in-kind (PIK) income has risen. These are classic indicators of tightening financial conditions.

Still, context matters. As of November 2025, for most borrowers, this reflects a normal adjustment to higher rates, not widespread distress. Defaults are rising, but from a very low base. As illustrated, the average interest coverage ratio has fallen meaningfully—yet remains above pre-pandemic levels.

Average interest coverage ratio and share of PIK income across BDCs.

Stress contained within private markets

Despite localized strains, there’s little evidence private credit stress has spilled into public markets. Listed business development companies (BDCs)—a reasonable proxy for private credit performance—have softened modestly but stabilized in recent months. High-yield spreads, another important barometer, remain near their year-to-date averages.

Managers we engage report a similar story: A few challenged borrowers, limited contagion, and healthy levels of interest coverage overall. Transparency is improving as institutional allocators demand more detailed loan-level data and tighter documentation standards as well

Importantly, our recent survey of funded managers shows most private-credit GPs (general partners) report stable or improving portfolio quality. According to a review of the internal watchlist for each of our managers, the majority of GPs have actually seen declines in the number of monitored credits, and several managers currently have no watchlist positions. Our survey reported only isolated, sector-specific names that have shown any deterioration, with most managers indicating issues are not expected to lead to material losses.

Changes to watchlist composition from Russell Investments’ private credit managers

Credit fundamentals remain sound: Among managers surveyed, leverage averages roughly 4.9× Debt / TTM EBITDA and interest coverage about 2.3× across portfolios, leaving borrowers with adequate service cushions even under tighter financial conditions. We do see dispersion widening. Managers are conducting what many describe as “full portfolio scrubs,” reflecting a proactive stance toward credit surveillance and documentation discipline rather than reactive distress management.

In short, the system looks similar to isolated events in other markets rather than a “Bear Stearns moment” signaling systemic breakdown.

Please note while data and manager commentary are current as of November 2025, conditions may evolve.

Private credit proxies vs. high-yield spreads

Global high yield OAS

Watchpoints and the road ahead

While we believe current stresses are idiosyncratic, certain risk markers deserve continued attention. Liquidity constraints in semi-liquid vehicles, slower fundraising, and valuation disputes could amplify pressure if macro conditions tighten.

At the same time, the manager behavior shift revealed in our recent survey is notable: GPs are transitioning to forensic oversight, tightening credit standards and reassessing loan protections in specific sub-markets. This diagnostic approach underscores an industry that remains healthy but alert—focused on preserving value through disciplined monitoring rather than aggressive growth.

The chart below outlines these watchpoints—from borrower quality and funding liquidity to valuation discipline. Each represents a possible transmission channel if local cracks were to widen. For now, though, these remain monitoring points rather than catalysts for systemic stress.

Private Credit Risk Watchpoints

Borrowers & Collateral

  • Deterioration in interest coverage and free cash flow across portfolios.
  • Rising use of PIK, amend-and-extend, and liability-management deals.
  • Increasing number of opaque collateral, fraud, or double-pledging cases across similar structures.

Funding & Liquidity

  • Potential constraints on liquidity: gates or delays in semi-liquid private credit funds.
  • Challenges in fundraising activity.

Valuation

  • Sudden NAV cuts or restatements at major platforms.
  • Disputes over collateral quality or perfection of security.

We continue to monitor these areas closely.

Investor implications

For credit and fixed income investors, the message is one of cautious confidence. We believe recent defaults are isolated, not the start of a broader credit cycle downturn. Still, selective positioning and disciplined liquidity management are key.

We believe investors should focus on:

  • Diversifying across managers, vintages, and deal types to mitigate idiosyncratic exposure.
  • Prioritizing transparency in documentation and valuation practices.
  • Maintaining appropriate liquidity buffers within semi-liquid structures.

In our view, private credit remains an attractive income source — provided investors remain vigilant and active in monitoring the evolving landscape.

Important information pertaining to the hypothetical example: Past performance does not predict future returns. Return level is proportionately scaled in line with cash level to be overlaid. Source: Russell Investments. Assumptions: Average cash level 1.0%, 10-year history from 12/31/2023, gross of fees. Opportunity cost from not securitizing cash varies by asset allocation and time period, and is represented by horizontal bars as marked within the chart legend. Target asset allocation used: 0% cash, 74% MSCI World, 26% Global Aggregate (GBP Hedged). For illustrative purposes only. Does not represent any actual investment. Indexes are unmanaged and cannot be invested in directly. Performance benefit (Net) of overlaying cash by last 5 individual calendar year is as follows: 2023:20 bps, 2022:-17bps, 2021:16bps, 2020:14bps, 2019:23bps.

These views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and are current as of the date at the top of the page. The information, analysis, and opinions expressed herein are for general information only and are not intended to provide specific advice or recommendations for any individual or entity.

This material is not an offer, solicitation or recommendation to purchase any security.

Forecasting represents predictions of market prices and/or volume patterns utilizing varying analytical data. It is not representative of a projection of the stock market, or of any specific investment.

Nothing contained in this material is intended to constitute legal, tax, securities or investment advice, nor an opinion regarding the appropriateness of any investment. The general information contained in this publication should not be acted upon without obtaining specific legal, tax and investment advice from a licensed professional.

Diversification and strategic asset allocation do not assure a profit or guarantee against loss in declining markets.

Please remember that all investments carry some level of risk, including the potential loss of principal invested. They do not typically grow at an even rate of return and may experience negative growth.
