data.title: Arista: Cisco Revitalized Whilst Growth Rate Decelerates
data.description:
Arista Networks Inc. (ANET) is a data center networking company, providing high-performance Ethernet switches and its own software to ensure minimal latency and high connectivity between GPUs to avoid model sharding as much as possible. Model sharding happens when the massive workloads are distributed between multiple GPUs, so latency is increased due to the GPUs needing to talk to one another. The higher the memory bandwidth, the faster workloads can be transported across one GPU to another.

Arista is one of the key players in the AI boom as workloads are getting larger and larger, so the connectivity has to be high, consistent and stable to ensure latency is as low as possible. Arista has already overtaken Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) as the key player of data center networking infrastructure, so the question now is more focused on whether Arista can maintain its lead and whether it can continue to outpace Cisco. Cisco spiked post-earnings whilst Arista fell, so this article aims to evaluate how it is doing fundamentally and whether the premium against Cisco remains justified. Already trading at high valuations, there may be a better entry point for Arista despite the dip, as it is still trading at huge premiums to not just Cisco, but also the relative sector. Neither overbought nor oversold on the RSI YTD and 1Y scale, though closer to oversold, I'm rating Arista at a 'Hold'.

Arista and AI

The AI boom hasn't shown signs that it is slowing down soon, as AMD CEO Lisa Su mentioned that we are only in the 2nd year of a 10-year AI infrastructure buildout.

From their recent AMD Financial Analyst Day, AMD had their TAM for AI accelerators in 2030 projected for over $1T, with this year's TAM being around $200B. The demand for AI accelerators is projected to continue to ramp up over the coming years with a CAGR of 40%.

With huge capex numbers from top hyperscalers like $125B from Amazon in just 2025, what I'm trying to get to is that demand for Arista won't likely die down soon, as it continues to grow fast as they have only penetrated a small % of their TAM.

As Arista has overtaken Cisco in revenue and unit share since late 2023, they have been the leader in data center switching and significantly outperformed Cisco in terms of networking growth rate. The $105B TAM is their target for TAM by 2029, as the networking market continues to grow following the AI boom.

With Q4 guided at a midpoint of $2.35B, the full-year revenue would come out to $8.86B, meaning that Arista has only penetrated under 10% of their TAM.

With Arista as one of the top companies when it comes to networking, any signs of the AI boom continuing to accelerate should only signal positive sentiment for Arista, as demand will continue to accelerate. As one of the leaders in networking, there are 2 constant focus points when evaluating Arista. Whether the AI boom continues to accelerate and whether their position as one of the leaders of networking is compromised.

Arista and Cisco's Networking

A key thing whilst understanding the networking market is that Cisco, the legacy player, used to have a majority market share in networking. However, the script has been flipped in recent years, as Arista has a larger market share in revenue and unit share in the market now compared to in 2012, when Cisco dominated the market with over 70% market share in both revenue and unit share.

The reason why Arista edges over Cisco is due to Arista's software, Arista EOS. It is run on one operating system (Linux-based but still can be programmed by other layers), easier to automate and the most efficient high-performance Universal Cloud Network architecture in the market. Arista is also trying to make everything as modular as possible, with the main idea being that when a certain part of the switch doesn't work, the whole system and set of switches doesn't have to be restarted, which contrasts with Cisco, being more monolithic, so a restart would lead to the huge system resetting, increasing lag time and potential delays. They are trying to fix this problem, and the approach is allowing more modular functions, but the legacy Cisco continues to lag behind Arista as evidenced by the respective growth rates of both firms.

Now onto the most recent quarter of earnings for both Cisco and Arista, Cisco surged post-earnings with optimism in their networking segment, whilst Arista's stock fell with analysts' suspicion that the growth rate may be slowing down with lower sequential order QoQ. Cisco in FY25 had its networking revenue fall 2% YoY, but Q1 FY26 showed 15% YoY growth in its networking segment, which perhaps signalled to investors that there is an acceleration in revenue growth, which is a problem for Arista, as Cisco is getting more competitive again. Though Cisco's outlook looked bullish with higher guidance, I don't think Arista is losing ground just yet and investors need to understand the bigger picture. These are some Cisco numbers:

All % refers to YoY growth Networking segment growth Product ARR Product RPO
Q1 FY26 15% 7% 10%
Q1 FY25 (23%) 42% 24%
Q1 FY24 10% 10% 14%

I believe the main reason Cisco surged post earnings was due to the narrative change with negative growth for networking in the prior fiscal year. They beat earnings with their security segment (Splunk) performing poorly in this quarter, so investors were bullish for the rest of the fiscal year for Cisco. Here is why this isn't a huge deal for Arista, in my opinion. Cisco in Q1 FY24 had networking revenue at $8.8B, whilst this quarter had $7.77B. Taking a step back, Cisco revenue fell 11.7% from Q1 FY24 to Q1 F6, whilst Arista went from $1.5B in revenue in Q3 2023 to $2.3b in Q3 2025, which is a 53.3% increase across 2 years.

So from taking a step back and looking at their respective revenues, Arista grew over 50% in 2 years whilst Cisco declined over 10% in 2 years. Cisco's revenue numbers value-wise are way larger than Arista's, but Arista continues to gain ground despite the 'huge narrative change' from the most recent quarter. Fundamentally, Arista is still doing very well, whilst Cisco is still playing catch-up in these years. With the huge amount of capex from 2023 to today, Cisco's falling networking revenue should be a huge red flag to investors. Taking a look at their ARR and RPO, there was a huge growth in FY25, whilst there is now a slowdown in the start of FY26, which could suggest that this 'narrative change' may be short-lived as Arista's numbers continue to impress investors.

The edge Cisco continues to have over Arista is their connections as a legacy player as well as Splunk. With Cisco having connections with the federal government as well as collaborations with AI accelerators, names like AMD with embedded Cisco switches, it is clear that despite leading to Cisco right now, the recent downtrend for Cisco may have been the bottom and a rebound may be in place for Cisco. Investors in Arista should heavily monitor Cisco's numbers and collaborations as despite the trend being Cisco down and Arista up, this quarter has slightly changed the narrative that Cisco isn't completely out of the picture just yet. With Splunk's ARR and RPO growing double digits and a larger shift to more cloud subscriptions, despite Arista appealing as the best cloud networking switches out there, Cisco's is appealing through offering bundles and cybersecurity, which is a huge part of the whole AI ecosystem today and a focus of the government as well, which Cisco has ties to for decades.

Evidence from Cisco's earnings call:

We continue to see strong performance from Splunk, closing one of our largest Splunk deals to date in Q1, enabled by joint Cisco and Splunk sales engagement. Splunk's ARR and product RPO grew double digits as we saw a notable change in how customers consume Splunk offerings in Q1 with a shift to more cloud subscriptions and fewer on-premise deals.

But as it relates to the end of support stuff that happens to be in the -- particularly in the federal government, there is a lot of discussion and a lot of pressure beginning to build on ensuring that, that equipment gets updated just from a cyber risk perspective and a hygiene perspective. So we would expect that there'll be some upside from that as they get back and begin working again and reopening the government.

Arista Q3

I believe the main reason for the recent sell-off was due to lower growth numbers, which disappointed investors, whilst Cisco's numbers impressed investors. The guided $2.35B in revenue only suggests a 21.7% growth YoY, as well as lower sequential growth QoQ compared to the last 3 quarters. It seems like revenue was accelerating, but since Q2 with all the tariff fears, it appears that Arista's revenue growth has been slowing down, as the growth rate is decelerating.

With America's revenue accounting for 80% of revenue separated by geographical segment, it does seem that Arista's growth rate right after the huge tariff war has been affected revenue-wise.

With around a 20% growth rate for the next year, down from their 26%-27% growth for this year, it shows a huge deceleration in growth rate, as the trend does show that Cisco may be catching up to Arista, as the inflection point has essentially happened, and Cisco's comeback might've impacted Arista's growth rate.

Already trading at high valuations, especially a huge premium to Cisco, as Cisco had $14.88B in revenue to Arista's $2.31B in the most recent quarter, but Cisco doesn't even have double of Arista's market cap, it should be no surprise that any signs of slowing down will lead to a downward trend for the stock. With Cisco now back in the race, I argue the recent downtrend for Arista is justified and is now honestly up to their 2026.

Their Q4 guidance is already slightly disappointing, and assuming they do beat top and bottom lines, their guidance for 2026 is also very important as they are only guiding for around a 20% growth rate right now. If Q1 26 is guided at exactly a 20% growth rate (2.4B for Q1 26), and Q4 25 comes out at $2.35B, that's a sequential QoQ growth of only 2%, which is even more disappointing than this quarter with sequential growth of 5%, which is already disappointing with the prior quarter at 10%.

From their earnings call:

But as we go to a significant scale-up volume, we expect more margin and economic capability coming together. In other words, the volume of these things will be larger, and the pressure on margins will be greater. So -- but we will carefully have a mix of scale up, scale out and scale across to not affect the overall margins, but definitely take our fair share in that.

On top of the slower growth rate, it is also likely that their margins will take a hit. From 2025 gross and operating margin at ~64% and ~48%, these numbers will fall in 2026 to ~62%-64% and ~43%-45%. Despite still having very impressive numbers, it does seem like the trend shows decelerating growth and contracting margins, as competition is getting more and more fierce as the AI boom continues to run.

Arista changing their guidance in the next quarter with higher projections can very much change the narrative back to less deceleration for Arista, as we can get a better picture for next year in the next quarter, but investors should remain cautious as Arista itself has already raised some caution themselves with these projections.

Valuation

Despite an over 20% correction in stock price already, Arista continues to trade at a premium to Cisco as well as the relative AI sector. It is trading at a significant premium to its 5-year historical average, as the company may be more mature now and revenue starts slowing down, perhaps an over 20% premium for P/E and P/S may not be justified.

Taking a look at P/E and P/S with Arista and Cisco, Arista trades at a higher premium by over 50% for P/E and over 3x for P/S. The question now for investors should be whether Arista is overvalued or whether Cisco is undervalued. With Cisco already trading at high premiums to the relative sector, higher than the sector median for both P/E and P/S, it is easier to argue that Arista is overvalued rather than that Cisco is undervalued relative to each other. Arista continues to do well, but with slower growth rates now and uncertain numbers for the future, it is hard to determine whether such a premium to Cisco is justified, especially looking at the P/S level.

Looking at Arista on the RSI scale, it is now near the oversold level, which has only been approached twice in the last year, during the initial March sell-off as well as the April lows. With no clear trend that Arista is undervalued, as valuations remain high despite being oversold, an entry point for Arista perhaps isn't very clear, especially since fundamentally it seems like the trend is now decelerating.

Final Thoughts

Arista continues to be one of the top preferred choices for networking solutions for data centers, as I believe that Arista will continue to lead over Cisco over the coming years. I'm bullish for the whole networking segment and the AI boom, but there is currently a small inflection point within the networking segment, where Cisco is getting revitalized and showing signs of growth, though still negative growth on a larger time scale, whilst Arista is showing signs of deceleration, whilst trading at high premiums. The next few quarters are important for Arista to determine the longer-term trend and whether Arista can flip to accelerating growth again, and whether Cisco can continue its re-acceleration trend will determine how competitive Cisco still is in the data center networking market. Until further news and guidance, I'm personally avoiding the networking market as the competition is getting more and more fierce, which will likely affect margins (seen with guided lower margins for 2026 than 2025), with a potential price war as both Cisco and Arista have their own unique differentiation point, Arista's software and Cisco's security.

data.title: Jobs: Every Month In 2025 Has Been Revised Down, With Two Turning Negative
data.description:
Note: This is the Jobs report for September that was released late on November 20th. There will be no October jobs report

The analysis below covers the Employment picture released on the first Friday of every month. While most of the attention goes to the Headline Report, it can be helpful to look at the details, revisions, and other reports to get a better gauge of what is really going on.

Current Trends

The jobs report showed a modest increase of 119k jobs. This month actually saw a big deviation in the Household Survey to the upside, which is quite rare, although it was the second month in a row this happened with 251k. The Household Survey had 288k jobs. In most months, the Household Survey is underperforming the headline number.

Figure 1: Primary Report vs. Household Survey - Monthly

For the year, the Household Survey is significantly higher due to the data anomaly in January. However, if we removed the data correction in January, the Household Survey would actually show a loss of 254k.

Figure 2: Primary Report vs. Household Survey - Annual

Switching to the headline report...

The BLS publishes the data behind their Birth/Death assumptions (formation of new business). In September, the assumption was actually negative by 144k. This is quite rare but was a headwind for the September jobs report.

Figure 3: Primary Unadjusted Report with Birth/Death Assumptions - Monthly

For the year, the birth/death assumption is positive 817k against a negative number for actuals (-1M). This means all positive growth YTD is directly tied to the birth/death assumptions.

Figure 4: Primary Unadjusted Report with Birth/Death Assumptions - Monthly

Digging Into the Headline Report

The 119k jobs number was accompanied by a rise in the unemployment rate to 4.4%.

Figure 5: Change by sector

Jobs by Category

When looking at the last 12-month trend, 3 of the categories were above trend, with 5 below (3 of which were negative).

Figure 6: Current vs. TTM

The table below shows a detailed breakdown of the numbers.

Figure 7: Labor Market Detail

Revisions

This is the biggest component of the jobs report. Every single month this year has seen negative revisions. May and June both saw +100k jobs come off the total, with June actually turning negative. Both June and August are now showing negative job months, where they had previously been positive.

Figure 8: Revisions

Over the last twelve months, jobs have been revised down by about 22k per month and revised lower by 53k over the last three months!

Figure 9: Revisions

More Detail in the Household Survey

Another level of detail in the Household report shows full-time vs. part-time job holders. This month shows that full-time jobs were gained, while part-time were lost

Figure 10: Full-Time vs. Part-Time

Historical Perspective

The chart below shows data going back to 1955.

Figure 11: Historical Labor Market

The labor force participation rate is still well below the highs before the Global Financial Crisis. This month, it increased slightly to 62.4%.

Figure 12: Labor Market Distribution

Conclusion

The jobs revisions are going nearly unnoticed by the overall market. Can you imagine how the market would have reacted had the initial release for June and August been negative? The labor market is very weak, and the inflation is elevated. This puts the Fed in a really bad spot. They are going to cut rates regardless - if not in December, then at the next meeting.

AI combined with an already weak labor market will lead to a tough 2026 for employment.
